common_name: River Oats (Native Shade-Tolerant Ornamental Grass with Nodding Flat Seed Heads)

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; River Oats self-sows prolifically in favorable conditions — allow it to naturalize in shade garden areas but prevent seeding into naturalized areas where it can spread aggressively beyond the garden.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; River Oats (Chasmanthium latifolium) is one of the most valuable and beautiful native ornamental grasses for Gulf South gardens — uniquely, it is one of the very few ornamental grasses that performs well in partial to full shade, filling the difficult niche of shade-tolerant ornamental grass that few other species can occupy. The slender, arching, bamboo-like stems carry flat, compressed, oat-like seed heads in an elegantly nodding arrangement — the flat seed heads rattle and shimmer in the slightest breeze, creating continuous gentle movement that adds life and animation to static shade plantings. The seed heads emerge green in summer and gradually ripen to warm tan-gold through fall, persisting on the plant into winter and providing months of ornamental interest. In fall, the leaves also develop warm copper-bronze tones before the plant goes dormant. River Oats is native to moist woodlands, stream banks, and shaded slopes throughout the eastern US, including Gulf Coast states, making it an ecologically appropriate native grass for Gulf South shade garden designs. It spreads by self-seeding and can naturalize enthusiastically in favorable moist conditions — this exuberant self-seeding can be welcome in large naturalistic areas but may require editing in smaller formal gardens. Deer tend to avoid it. An outstanding grass for woodland garden understory, shade border edges, and moist streamside gardens.

common_name: Gulf Muhly Grass (Native Pink-Purple Cloud of Fall Bloom Native Gulf Coast Grass)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gulf Muhly requires full sun for the best fall flowering — partial shade reduces flower production significantly and the spectacular fall cloud effect requires maximum sun exposure to fully develop.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Gulf Muhly (Muhlenbergia capillaris) is the fall blooming sensation of the Gulf South native grass world — one of the most spectacular flowering displays of any plant adapted to the Gulf Coast, producing enormous, airy, billowing clouds of pink to rose-purple flowering spikelets in September–November that completely envelop the fine-textured foliage clump, creating a soft, ethereal cloud-like mass of color that looks like pastel smoke billowing from the base of each plant. A well-established planting of Gulf Muhly in full fall bloom is genuinely breathtaking — the fine, mist-like texture of millions of tiny flower spikelets creates a color mass with a translucent, glowing quality, especially when backlit by morning or late-afternoon sun, that is unlike any other ornamental plant effect. Gulf Muhly is native to the coastal plain from Massachusetts to Florida and west to Texas, growing in dry, open, rocky or sandy soil in full sun — a classic Gulf South native adapted to the impoverished sandy and rocky soils of the region. The fine-textured, narrow green leaves form a graceful, arching clump through spring and summer, with no sign of the coming spectacular fall bloom until the flower stems begin to emerge in September. Drought-tolerant once established. Avoid fertilization — excess nitrogen reduces flowering and makes plants floppy. Cut back to 4–6 inches in late winter to encourage fresh growth.

common_name: David Verity Cuphea (Prolific Orange-Red Tubular Hummingbird Annual for Summer Heat)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; David Verity is a sterile hybrid that does not set seed — propagated vegetatively by cuttings and sold only as container plants rather than seeds.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; David Verity Cuphea is one of the most valuable and consistent hummingbird-attracting annuals for Gulf South summer gardens — a hybrid Cuphea (likely Cuphea ignea x C. llavea parentage) that produces an extraordinary abundance of small, tubular, bright orange-red flowers tipped with purple-black from spring through hard frost, creating a continuous nectar source that ruby-throated hummingbirds and butterflies find irresistible. The small, tubular flower form is precisely calibrated for hummingbird bill access — each flower produces concentrated nectar that makes these plants extremely effective hummingbird magnets in late summer and fall when migrating hummingbirds are actively fueling up for their Gulf of Mexico crossing. The flowers are produced so prolifically and continuously that established plants rarely appear to be between bloom flushes — the orange-red and black color combination creates a vivid display through the longest, hottest Gulf South summer days. 'David Verity' is sterile and propagated by cuttings, making it available only as nursery transplants. The compact, mounding to slightly spreading habit makes it excellent for container culture (where it can be overwintered as a tender perennial) and for summer border edge plantings. In zone 9, it grows as a warm-season annual from spring transplanting through first frost. In zone 10–12, it may survive winter as a subshrub. No deadheading required — spent flowers drop cleanly and new buds open continuously.

common_name: Tuscan Blue Rosemary (Tall Upright Culinary Rosemary with Deep Blue-Violet Flowers)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to dry, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Tuscan Blue is one of the most cold-sensitive rosemary cultivars among the upright types — it is rated to zone 7 but may be marginal in zone 7b and the colder portions of zone 8; in zone 9, it is reliably perennial.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, lean soil in full sun; Tuscan Blue is the classic tall, strongly upright rosemary cultivar — one of the most commonly grown culinary rosemaries in Gulf South herb gardens, selected for its vigorous, columnar-upright habit, robust culinary fragrance and flavor, and particularly vivid deep blue-violet flowers that are more intensely colored than many other rosemary cultivars. The tall, strictly upright habit (4–6 feet) makes Tuscan Blue distinctly different from spreading and prostrate rosemary forms, functioning as a narrow, evergreen vertical accent in herb and kitchen garden designs rather than a low-spreading groundcover. The strongly aromatic, narrow, needle-like leaves are the standard culinary rosemary used in Mediterranean cooking — with the same powerful resinous rosemary scent and flavor as the straight species. In Gulf South zone 9, Tuscan Blue grows as a long-lived evergreen perennial that thrives in the heat and dry conditions that characterize Mediterranean-climate herbs. Well-drained soil is the critical requirement: rosemary in heavy clay or poorly drained soil declines rapidly. The fragrant blue flowers in late winter (February–March in zone 9) provide important early nectar for honeybees and native bees emerging from winter dormancy. Tuscan Blue can be trained as a formal herb garden accent, clipped as a low hedge, or grown as a specimen shrub. Prune after flowering to maintain a compact habit; avoid cutting into old, woody, leafless stems.

common_name: Profusion Orange Zinnia (AAS-Winning Disease-Resistant Heat-Proof Single Zinnia)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is consistently warm (70°F+) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Profusion Orange was bred specifically to resist the powdery mildew that typically devastates standard large-flower zinnias (Z. elegans) in Gulf South summer humidity — plant it where standard zinnias have failed.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Profusion Orange is an All-America Selections winner — part of the Profusion zinnia series developed as a breakthrough in zinnia breeding for humid, heat-stressed climates where standard large-flowered zinnias (Zinnia elegans) historically succumb to powdery mildew and plant decline by midsummer. The Profusion series (Zinnia marylandica, a hybrid between Z. elegans and Z. angustifolia) combines the powdery mildew resistance and heat tolerance of the narrow-leafed zinnia (Z. angustifolia) with the flower size and color range of Z. elegans, producing smaller but abundant, clean single flowers on a compact, bushy plant that maintains healthy foliage through Gulf South summer conditions that devastate regular zinnias. Profusion Orange produces warm orange single flowers (2–2.5 inch) with golden-yellow centers, creating a compact mass of color from planting through frost without the white powdery mildew coating that eventually ruins Z. elegans plantings in humid conditions. The self-cleaning habit (dropping spent flowers without deadheading) and continuous bloom production from a relatively small, mounding plant make it practical for garden edges, container planting, and cutting. Monarch butterflies and swallowtails visit the flowers extensively. For maximum Gulf South reliability, the Profusion series is one of the most consistently recommended zinnia alternatives to the otherwise problematic large-flowered types.
